title: The Darling
excerpt: “See you soon, darling.”—Last transmission of Pilot Huntz.
body:
# Prayer before Jump

They still touch the bulkhead before a Hyperjump. Two fingertips, quick as a cough. The Intuits call it knocking on the pane—the thin place where distance loosens and numbers consider their teeth. The gesture doesn’t change the math; it steadies the people who must trust it.

Huntz touched the pane because his father had. He flew a blue-white courier, the Pipistrelle, hauling small urgencies: cultures, ledger coils, the odd velvet box. Huntz liked his coffee to taste like burnt paper. He kept two old shepherds to keep the silence civil - and because he was a touch sentimental. Before launch he pinged the portmaster, messaged his wife, and set his jaw against a clatter in the starboard bulkhead he kept meaning to tighten. His wife messaged him back, 'see you soon, my darling.' He had never in his life called her darling. She'd teased him for years about that - how he could write a symphony about engine harmonics, but couldn't say a sugary word without mumbling. The subtle jest of a couple comfortable with who they were. 

---

Imperial Decree 77-A: Hyperjump Safety Advisory
Office of Continuity

> Maintain schedules. Unauthorized rumor of 'warpspace predation' disrupts logistics and will be penalized. Hyperjump remains statistically optimal for empirewide supply. Report anomalies through University relays. Avoid sensational language.

Stamped in red and hung beside docking fees and a card that read: FAST CASH FOR SPARE PARTS.

---

The fold took him clean. Lights thinned to arithmetic. No one had expected that Hyperspace would look just like they had imagined in those old films. He let the solution path he’d drafted while the universe was still ordinary unspool itself. It wasn't sixteen seconds before the ship’s alarm arranged itself, as if someone had tidied the room and was waiting to be thanked. Hair rose in a precise line along his arm. The dogs went statue-still.

“Show me the portside,” he said.

“Sh0w m3 the portsidee,” something said back, a fraction behind—his timbre, his breath, his timing.

So it was true, he thought grimly, my time has come. Still, he stopped spending syllables. He kept thought behind his teeth. He imagined the ship as it was, every nook and cranny. In his head, the safe corridor was a narrow, lit dock; beyond it, water with no name for how cold it was. He chided himself. Don’t admire the dark.

---

Training Script, Unseen General
Lesson 4: Silence Is Cheap, Syllables Are Expensive

> If ever in the course of navigation, you feel a drop—a noise where no noise should be—do not blink and do not name. Follow the solution you plotted before fear. Hold reality tight in your mind. Do not spend words.

A thumbprint smudges the margin where someone almost wrote a name.

---

At twenty seconds the noise tidied closer. The shepherds did not whine; they had a word for adventure and another for boredom. They had none for this. Huntz tightened his mind, the mind he had trusted his whole life, the mind which made him a pilot.

 Two entire minutes he lasted. Then, he thought of his wife smoothing his hair with the back of her hand, her laugh when he ducked. That was when the noise learned tenderness all by itself, 'l0ve'.

---

Hyperjump Temporal Beacon-Δ
Vessel: Pipistrelle

> 00:00:03 — Entry nominal.
> 00:00:09 — Pilot(?) exhalation resembling laughter.
> 00:00:12 — Canines: sustained alert posture, no vocalization.
> 00:00:15 — Broadband noise collapses to low-variance field.
> 00:00:16 — Pilot(?): "Show me the port—"
> 00:00:21 — [data degradation]
> 00:00:31 — Interval; pattern resembles web closing (analyst’s metaphor).
> 00:02:00 - Unknown: "Engine, ledger, Pipistrelle, resin."
> 00:02:10 — Pilot(?): "See you soon, darling."
> Re-manifest: none.

---

PI Whiteleaf, Practical Notes on Jump Edges, marginalia

> Folktale is a reflection of truth. "Spider" = high-dimensional trap topology. "Web" = torsion congruences. Stop laughing at the word spider.

---

Spousal Statement

> He never called me darling.

When she received the recording from the leadership, she listened just once. She didn't cry. It was a promise spoken in a voice that arrived half a heartbeat before it was made. After, the word soon never meant soon again; it meant coolant humming under the floor.

---

Dock Six learned the news the way warm things move in winter: quickly, quietly, and upwards. The pane-knock grew sharper, like people checking a latch twice. Someone scratched a new line into the prayer; someone else crossed it out. A steward taped over a hand-lettered warning with a fee schedule.

---

Trader Ledger, Master Calabash
Lot 4412: Scar-haul, Far Halcyon

> Chartbook fused to glass; ring ovalized; vacuum suit chewed—not cut. Purchaser paid in cash and a prayer.

The Trader didn’t ask who the prayer was for. Prayers keep their value if you don’t mark them to market.

---

Imperial Decree 77-B (Addendum):

> Risk index unchanged. Maintain throughput. Dockmasters to counsel crews and discourage theatricality. Repeat offenders for rumor-mongering may forfeit hazard bonuses. Keep lanes open.

---

Huntz’s route was reassigned. The berth went to the one known as a wayfinder. Someone finally tightened the starboard clatter. The dogs found a mechanic who didn’t like silence either. Freight moved: grain and oxygen and tax and letters from people who never use the word darling. Most ships return. The ones that don’t become part of the Ruins, and the economy remembers them the only way it knows how—by arriving elsewhere on time.

title: Viceroy Isha
excerpt: A wise Emperor maintains a powerful Viceroy, of course.
body:
# The Empire Still Breaths

The morning bells of Era Imperial still sound like triumph. Brass throats pour sunlight into the plazas; banners move as if the wind salutes. But the marble steps are patched with resin, and the crowds learn to cheer between ration hours. In the great shadow of the Palace, Viceroy Isha signs three hundred orders before breakfast - none of them in his own name.

He is careful with ceremony. White-gloved clerks bring the Sun Seal on a velvet cushion; the Moon Seal follows like an echo. Isha warms the wax with a candle cupped in his palm, blows out the flame, and lowers the Emperor’s signet as if settling a child to sleep. The parchment takes the weight. The Empire exhales.

---

### The Hall of Signatures

The Hall is a long nave of black basalt and inlaid brass, its ceiling a calendar of victories The Empire can no longer afford to fight. Beneath those stars, life is arithmetic: ships per day, grain per soul, kilowatt-hours per moving crowd. Isha’s pen is a metronome for a civilization in 4/4 time.

He drafts the Edict of Continuance:

- Relief prices fixed at last year’s levels—for appearance. 
- Dock stipends doubled—for quiet. 
- Procession routes lengthened—for faith. 

It is not malice; it is maintenance. The double speak required to keep things just the way they are - in imperfect stability.

---

### An Audience of Two

The Emperor arrives late to his own private audience, perfume preceding him. He is pale and beautifully costumed, a sonnet in silk. He asks after the fountains; he praises the taste of a new sugar that never reached the docks.

“I will speak to those meddling Foundries,” he says, bright as a coin.

“You already have,” Isha answers. He slides the folio near the Emperor’s idle hand. “Your words were most persuasive.”

The Emperor smiles with relief—that ancient, imperial gratitude for work already done—and presses the seal without reading. There is kindness in Isha’s bow, only because there is something unfeeling behind it, like iron fitted beneath velvet.

---

### The Trader with the Dust on Her Boots

Xenia of the Guild comes to court without apology. Asteroid Belt dust rims her cuffs; a ledger-cuff clicks softly when she flexes her wrist.

“I'll keep this brief,” she says. “Tariffs on lots are strangling our crews. We can move bread faster than your... parades. Let us, and we’ll keep your lines straight.”

“Straight lines break,” Isha says, not unkindly. “We prefer curves.”

She watches the seals, not the man. Without missing a beat, “Your curve meets my margin at 10%.” Tap. “Lower the premium on high-risk jumps. We’ll take the lanes you won’t insure. You get a quiet dock; I take the scrap and the glory.”

“Glory is costly,” Isha murmurs. There was something about her voice. As a master of persuasion himself, he could sense it was unnatural. But he felt that sense slipping away, an alarm faded into the background noise of city life. “But cheaper than panic.”

In the afternoon dispatch he writes:

> On the Emperor’s Authority: Effective immediately, premiums on frontier salvage reduced by one-fifth for vessels under Guild oath. The Guild to be the sole provider for food tonnage to capitals at festival cadence.

Xenia bows the way explorers bow: like people who live with weight. They both know the bargain would last only a month.

---

### Scholars in the Bright Rooms

The Scholars have no banners, no guilds, only charts and exams. Their halls smell of chalk and late tea; they are an eclectic bunch of scientists, "scientists", and bureaucrats. A striking young man presents a sequence that stretches beyond the muraled ceiling.

“Observe the curves,” he says. “A soft landing built from small surrenders. Sell monopolies to buy time. Local auctions for civic permits. Price the rumor before it becomes revolt.”

“Price the rumor?” Isha asks.

“A stipend for silence is less dear than cavalry,” he says. “Numbers do not love ceremony.”

“And silence keeps us all from being stabbed in the street,” Isha replies. Subtlety tired him today - a worrying sign. Still, he signs grants for the scholar's new lab. You do not argue with those who can measure reality; you invite them to dinner and serve smaller plates. Isha was sure the young man before him would rise the ranks quickly.

---

### The Whispering Lanes

Rumors travel faster than couriers. There is talk of men who do more than steer ships: Navigators who can steer a man by half a breath; who leave a soft pressure at the end of your sentence, and make the next word arrive more amicably.

Isha meets one in a quiet garden, a woman in plain gray with a stare like cooled metal. She holds her hands behind her back.

“I help ships survive the fold,” she says.

“And empires?” Isha asks. 

She does not smile. “Empires have stronger hulls.” A pause, perfectly measured. “Most days.”

She does not say yes; he does not ask again. But the Emperor’s speeches become shorter and kinder, and once—only once—His Majesty thanks the Viceroy by name. Isha files the change in a mental cabinet labeled Useful Myths.

---

### Bread, Brass, and Theater

Decay makes its own culture. Processions grow longer as budgets grow thin. The Capitol adds another ballroom; the people cannot dance. A choir sings of the old manufacturing wealth; their harmony hides what the ledgers can’t.

Isha never confuses applause with admiration. He orders the gilding of the inner gates not to impress the crowds but to reassure the working clerks who count the grain. If the gate shines, the numbers feel believed.

From the balcony he sees the city like a solved equation: avenues as vectors, markets as nodes, people as moving sums in need of balance. It is not cruel to see it that way, he tells himself; it is how an empire lasts another day.

---

### A Day of Three Threats

Morning: a Trader stoppage at Dyson Gate. Barges idle; unmoving. Dita does not send word. He does not need to.

Noon: Scholars issue a pamphlet with a calm title and violent implications—On the Inevitable Sector. It argues pensions should be scheduled by auction, not decree. The margins bloom with numbers like tiny knives.

Evening: the rumor of the men-who-adjust-minds arrives in the homes. Quacks tie charms to ladles; a wife forgets her own name and becomes a media darling. The Palace shrinks by an inch, as if breathing in.

Isha writes three orders by Imperial Seal:

1. Salvage tariffs shall float with the days of stoppage cured, and the Guild earns the right of first refusal at the next auction. (He hears Dita’s laugh in the ink.)
2. The University shall receive patronage, on condition they publish on “instruments of civic cohesion.” (They will accept the money and keep the knives.)
3. Navigators formally recognized as a sacred craft; no inquiries into methods permitted without Palace summons. (A velvet noose tightens around the media.)

The city rests. Not because anyone is satisfied, but because everyone has been seen on paper.

---

### The Quiet Arithmetic of Power

At night, Isha dines alone. He eats simply. He reads the day backward until it justifies itself. 

He believes three things:

- The Empire is not a crown; it is a calendar. 
- People forgive what arrives on time. 
- Collapse will come one day, but only after I am gone.

He files copies of all three beliefs in the Archives. He imagines future clerks opening them and nodding, the way you nod at a well-balanced account.

Outside, the capitol is beautiful. Beauty during prosperity is gaudy. Beauty during decay is strategic.

---

### A Final Signature

Dawn returns with its brass choir. The Emperor sleeps late and will wake with praises on his tongue that are not entirely his. Dita will bring him a coin stamped from ship’s hull and ask him to barter; he will laugh and pocket it. The Scholars will print a kinder paper. The Navigators will fold a thousand ships through angles no priest can bless.

And Isha—charismatic, careful, conniving—will sign the morning’s breath into law: not to be loved, not even to be thanked, but to keep a world walking between pillars that wobble by the hour.

He does not taste triumph. He tastes ink and warm wax and the quiet relief of machinery that refuses to die.

He lowers the seal. The Empire breathes again.

title: Frontier Surveyor
excerpt: "He thinks he's hiding. He's just creating a more interesting map."—Surveyor Marlow, field marginalia
body:
# Calibration

Marlow surveyed on foot, because instruments trust legs more than wheels. She carried a plumb bob, a spirit level, and a notebook that had outlived four pairs of boots. The frontier did not misbehave often, but it misbehaved consistently. In Tract 9, a plumb bob would not hang true. It leaned east by eleven degrees, then east by fourteen, then — two weeks later, in a different valley entirely — east by eleven again. *Rock does not migrate*, she wrote. Underlined twice.

She found him by accident, the way most important things are found. A boulder the size of a modest chapel, sitting in a field that owed it nothing. The local wheat bent toward it in a slow green spiral. A dropped coin took four seconds to fall a foot. She measured this twice, because once is an anecdote and twice is data.

Then the boulder said, without moving, "You're the third this month to measure me. The first two prayed. You didn't."

"Should I have?"

"No," he said. "Praying is boring. I like new things."

She named him Denny before she had permission, on the theory that no one gets to keep a title that heavy without paying some kind of tax on it.

---

The Plumb Line, Book of Weights, verse recited at every shrine threshold

> Do not measure the Anchor. Measurement is a lesser form of worship, practiced by those without the patience for devotion. Kneel; do not calibrate.

A footnote, added later in different ink: *Surveyors have been observed doing both at once. This is heresy. It is also, apparently, effective.*

---

College Fieldcraft, Vol. II — "On Reluctant Subjects"

> When a mass declines to be found, do not chase the mass. Chase the disturbance it leaves behind — the bent stalk, the slow coin, the compass that apologizes before it lies. Let the subject discover that being interesting is not the same as being unsafe, and that you are, happily, neither a threat nor a supplicant.

Marlow's hand in the margin: *He moves at night and pretends he doesn't know I've noticed. I pretend I haven't noticed him pretending. This is either courtship or a peer-reviewed methodology. Possibly both, pending review.*

---

Instrument Log — Tract 14, Night Survey

> 21:02 — Gravimeter reads +0.3g anomalous, bearing north.
> 21:19 — Anomaly stationary. Waiting.
> 21:44 — Anomaly relocated eight meters without displacing topsoil. Impossible. Logged anyway.
> 22:10 — Subject addressed observer directly: "You're loud when you sneak."
> 22:11 — Observer: "You're heavy when you tiptoe."
> 22:30 — No further anomalies recorded. Observer's notebook contains a small, unscientific sketch of a boulder wearing a hat. Not included in the official report.

---

Marginalia, Prof. Iset Cray, on Marlow's survey draft

> Your data is sound. Your footnotes are not. A gravimeter reading does not require the phrase "and then he laughed, which sounded like scree settling, and it was not unpleasant." Cut it.

*It was not cut.*

---

Found, scratched into slate near the north ridge, believed to be his hand

> You'll never publish this. I move before the ink sets.
> That's not a complaint.

---

Relic Broker's Ledger, entry 118

> Offered forty crown for "a chip off the Anchor, authenticated." Customer left with nothing. The stone does not shed. In thirty years of trade I have never met a resource less interested in being extracted.

---

Tarn's Rest, notice board

> Rooms available on the anomaly side, premium rate. Guests report small objects settle two inches left overnight. No refunds for romantic disappointment.

---

The Plumb Line, Addendum, posted at the shrine gate

> The Anchor has been sighted in company with a cartographer of no faith. Devotees are reminded that the sacred does not require an audience, and certainly not one carrying instruments instead of incense. Prayers continue as scheduled.

---

Marlow finished the survey of Tract 14 nine months late, which the College forgave, because the map she turned in was, by any measure, the most interesting one they'd received in a decade — full of contour lines that curved for no geological reason anyone could name except her. She never did catch him standing still. She stopped trying around the time she noticed she no longer wanted to. A caught thing stops being surveyed. It just sits there, finished, like everything else that's done.

These days the anomaly still drifts through her tracts, always eleven degrees off true, always somewhere she hasn't quite mapped yet. She updates the notebook. He moves the wheat. Neither of them has closed the distance, and both of them, by now, know exactly how far it is — which is, they've each separately decided, the correct distance for two bodies who'd rather keep orbiting than ever finish the paper.

title: Ace Navigator Telles
excerpt: "The odds are a million to one. A perfect hand."—Ace Navigator Telles, at the table
body:
Red's called itself a gentlemen's club and had exactly one gentleman in it, and he was cheating at solitaire in the corner because nobody would deal him in anymore. Everyone else came for the cards, the whiskey that tasted like it had opinions, and the particular kind of quiet that meant nobody in the room was going to ask your name.

Telles liked it for the same reasons, plus one: at Red's, nobody looked twice at a woman who kept her hair combed low over her brow. Half the regulars had scars they didn't explain either.

She had three cards face down in front of her and hadn't looked at them yet. She didn't need to. The knowing arrived the way it always did — a pressure behind her browline, patient and specific, the same sense that used to tell her, back when she still wore the uniform, exactly which half-second of a hyperjump not to breathe in. Her instructors at the College called it aptitude. Her mother had called it a gift. Telles, four years and one warrant into calling it neither, called it a headache with excellent night vision.

Across the table, Bosch was sweating through his collar. Bosch owed money to people who did not accept apologies as currency, and he'd bet the deed to a scow he didn't legally own yet in an attempt to fix that, which was the kind of math Telles could have told him wouldn't work even without opening her cards. She didn't tell him. Some educations you have to pay for yourself.

"You gonna look at your hand," Bosch said, "or just menace it?"

"Building suspense. You should be grateful. It's the only thing making this game interesting."

She turned the cards over. Three of a kind, kings, and she could feel the fourth sitting two cards deep in the shoe like it had left her a note. She could win this in the next four minutes without trying. That was, itself, the problem. Telles had learned the hard way that a woman who won every hand at every table got remembered, and a woman who got remembered got reported, and a woman who got reported got a very polite visit from the College asking why a decommissioned asset was drawing crowds in a border-moon card room instead of standing on a bridge where the Empire's remaining functional minds belonged.

So she did what she always did. She played it human. She held a beat too long on a bluff. She let her eyes flick toward the door like she was worried, even though the itch behind her brow had already confirmed nobody interesting was coming through it. She lost the small hands on purpose, the way a good con artist loses — visibly, believably, a little stupidly — so the big ones wouldn't look like a magic trick when she finally took them.

She'd learned that trick from a woman named Aveline, three fugitive years and one dead ship ago, in the back room of a card house on a moon Telles no longer said the name of out loud. *You don't fold because you're weak, girl,* Aveline used to say, tapping the discard pile like it was a shrine. *You fold because the Gray Lady's watching, and she likes to see you leave something on the table. Pure winners don't get old in this line of work.* Telles had always thought it was superstition. She still thought so. She still did it every night anyway, the way sailors keep knocking wood long after they've stopped believing in the wood.

Bosch shoved everything he had into the middle on a pair of eights he clearly believed in more than he should have.

Telles looked at her kings again. The odds are a million to one, she thought. A perfect hand.

Then she folded it anyway.

The table groaned. Bosch whooped and swept the pot toward himself like it might change its mind.

That was when the man at the end of the bar set his glass down without drinking from it — the kind of detail her training had taught her to notice a full second before her gift bothered to confirm it. He'd been nursing that whiskey for forty minutes. He hadn't come for the whiskey.

He crossed the room with the unhurried confidence of someone who got paid whether or not he hurried, and set a College warrant card on the felt, face up, beside the discard pile where her folded kings still sat.

"Warrant Officer Denholm. Navigator Registry, Recovery Division. You've been hard to find, Telles."

"I've been easy to find. You've been slow."

He didn't smile at that, which she respected. "The Empire's short eleven active navigators this quarter. Two jump-ships are grounded at Coraline Yard because there's nobody left who can read the math without a computer doing the guessing, and the computers have started guessing wrong. You know what that makes you? Not a fugitive. A shortage."

"Flattering. Still not applying."

"You fold hands you could win. I watched you do it twice tonight before I even sat down." He nodded at the pile. "Bosch doesn't beat kings. Not with that itch you've got. So either you're the worst player in this room, or you're exactly what the file says, and I'd like to know which before I have to do this the way I don't want to."

Telles glanced at the folded kings, face-down, four cards ahead of the one that would have made them unbeatable. She thought about Aveline, and the Gray Lady, and the particular kind of freedom that only exists in the space between a hand you could win and a hand you let go.

"You're right," she said. "I could've taken that pot."

"So why didn't you?"

She pushed back from the table, unhurried, and reached up to brush the hair back from her brow — just long enough for Denholm to see exactly what he'd come for, and exactly why he was never walking her out of Red's tonight.

"Because I already know how tonight ends," she said, "and it isn't with you."

By the time Denholm's hand found his sidearm, she was through the back, past the gentleman still losing to himself at solitaire, out into the kind of dark a good navigator reads like a native tongue.

Behind her, Bosch was already bragging to the table about the hand that beat a navigator's kings. He'd tell that story for years. He'd get every card right and the winner wrong every single time.
